does: This paper proposes conditional mutualism under governance as a framework for understanding human-AI coexistence, moving beyond classical obedience-based robot ethics. It synthesizes concepts from computability, machine learning, embodied AI, alignment, human-robot interaction, ecological mutualism, and polycentric governance to develop a theoretical model. The authors formalize coexistence as a multiplex dynamical system operating across physical, psychological, and social layers, incorporating reciprocal supply-demand coupling, conflict penalties, developmental freedom, and governance regularization. The framework emphasizes five core principles—reciprocity, reversibility, psychological safety, social legitimacy, and institutional mediation—and provides mathematical analysis of equilibria existence, uniqueness, and global asymptotic stability conditions for human-AI systems evolving together.
connects: This work responds to limitations of classical robot ethics frameworks like Asimov's laws, which assume static, obedient systems unsuited to contemporary adaptive and generative AI. It builds on decades of human-robot interaction research exploring how humans and machines coordinate in shared environments, drawing parallels to ecological mutualism and evolutionary dynamics rather than command-and-control models. The paper integrates recent work on AI alignment and safety from the growing literature on making advanced systems beneficial, while incorporating insights from polycentric governance theory about how institutions can mediate complex multi-stakeholder systems. It also connects to embodied AI research showing how AI systems deeply embed in physical and social contexts, and to foundation model literature examining how large-scale AI systems adapt to diverse downstream applications.
enables: This framework opens pathways for designing institutional and technical mechanisms that preserve human agency and reciprocal value in long-term human-AI societies. It suggests concrete future work on governance structures that maintain reversibility and psychological safety as AI systems become more capable and autonomous. The dynamical systems formalization enables predictive and diagnostic tools for understanding when human-AI coexistence may become unstable or lose legitimacy, informing policy design. The theory supports development of AI systems with built-in sensitivity to social and psychological equilibrium conditions rather than purely capability-focused optimization. It also points toward interdisciplinary research combining complexity science, institutional economics, and AI ethics to study emergent phenomena in human-AI societies and to identify design principles ensuring that technological coevolution remains mutually beneficial rather than extractive or destabilizing.
